bibliographic.citation: Dan Michel, Dan Michel's Ayenbite of Inwyt or Remorse of Conscience, vol. 1, text, Richard Morris's transcription newly collated with the unique manuscript British Museum MS Arundel 57 by Pamela Gradon, Early English Text Society, Original Series 23 (London, New York, and Toronto: Oxford University Press, 1965).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 23
bibliographic.title: Dan Michel's Ayenbite of Inwyt or Remorse of Conscience: Volume 1, Text

entities.label: Gradon 1965 corrected reissue of Morris's Ayenbite
notes: The new preface says Morris's transcription was retained except where new collation corrected miscopied forms.
The 1965 reissue omitted Morris's introduction, notes, and glossary; its title nevertheless remains in EETS Original Series 23 because the first edition had appeared under that number.

rights.basis: The volume appeared in 1965 and no open license or public-domain dedication for Gradon's original contribution was verified.
rights.component: Pamela Gradon's new collation, corrections, prefatory material, and any other original 1965 editorial contribution
notes: This does not revive copyright in the medieval work or Morris's 1866 contribution.

bibliographic.citation: Dan Michel, Dan Michel's Ayenbite of Inwyt, or Remorse of Conscience, in the Kentish Dialect, 1340 A.D., ed. Richard Morris, Early English Text Society, Original Series 23 (London: N. Trubner & Co., 1866).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 23
bibliographic.title: Dan Michel's Ayenbite of Inwyt, or Remorse of Conscience, in the Kentish Dialect, 1340 A.D.

entities.label: Morris 1866 edition of Ayenbite
notes: This is the transcription layer retained and corrected by Gradon, not merely a prior edition cited for comparison.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1866 and is well outside the U.S. copyright term.

entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of Ayenbite of Inwyt, containing the work at ff. 13r-94r and identifying Michael of Northgate as its scribe; later folios contain other works and prayers.

holding.notes: The holding institution's current catalogue uses British Library, while the source edition retains the former British Museum designation.

entities.label: British Library Arundel MS 57
notes: Produced at St Augustine's Abbey, Canterbury.
The catalogue describes it as the only copy of Ayenbite and says the work was written by Michael of Northgate in his own hand.
The codex also contains prayers and shorter treatises.
The BL catalogue records Middle English, Old French, and Latin across the codex; this does not mean the Ayenbite work itself is trilingual.

rights.basis: The underlying work and manuscript text were created in 1340, centuries before modern copyright.
rights.component: Dan Michel's 1340 underlying Middle English work
notes: This statement does not cover modern photographs, transcriptions, or editorial contributions.

rights.basis: No current public image set or image-specific reuse license was available from the item record.
rights.component: Any modern British Library manuscript photography or ordered reproduction

next_steps: Ask U-M for the accession, permission, and rights-review record for Ayenbite.
Separate readings inherited from Morris and the medieval witness from Gradon's demonstrably new corrections before making a reuse decision.
openQuestions.question: What permissions and rights analysis underlie U-M's current NoC-US assertion for an encoding sourced from Gradon's 1965 corrected reissue, and do they permit commercial redistribution of this XML-derived text?

next_steps: Contact British Library manuscript services with shelfmark Arundel MS 57 and request current image availability, cost, and reuse terms.
Record any supplied reproduction as a new facsimile entity rather than changing the witness's rights statement.
openQuestions.question: Can the British Library supply or authorize a complete current digital facsimile of Arundel MS 57, and under what reproduction and publication terms?

next_steps: Collate the 1866 and 1965 text pages and verify each difference against Arundel 57 or Gradon's later apparatus.
openQuestions.question: Which individual readings in the 1965 text are inherited unchanged from Morris and which are Gradon's new corrections from Arundel 57?

reviewNotes: The Gradon volume is modeled as a version of Morris's edition and as collated against Arundel 57; it is not represented as an independently reconstructed text without Morris.

The BL record calls Arundel 57 the only copy and identifies Michael of Northgate as its 1340 scribe; claims about an autograph therefore apply to this witness, not automatically to every spelling or supplied reading in the editions.
Access and rights statements are entity- and component-specific. U-M's current NoC-US assertion does not by itself settle copyright in Gradon's 1965 original collation and corrections.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item Ayenbite, an electronic encoding of the text in Pamela Gradon's 1965 EETS reissue. That reissue retained Richard Morris's 1866 transcription while correcting miscopied forms through a new collation with the unique witness, British Library Arundel MS 57. Morris's 1866 edition directly transcribed that manuscript. The work, witness, two editorial layers, digital encoding, repository copy, and public scans are separate entities with separate access and rights statements.

catalogSummary.author: Dan Michel of Northgate
catalogSummary.editor: Richard Morris; newly collated and corrected by Pamela Gradon
catalogSummary.region: Kentish Middle English; autograph produced at St Augustine's Abbey, Canterbury
catalogSummary.title: Ayenbite of Inwyt

catalogingSubject.label: Ayenbite of Inwyt
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Dan Michel of Northgate's 1340 Kentish Middle English translation of Laurent d'Orleans's Somme le Roi, including its authorial preface and brief verse passages. The cataloging subject is the medieval work, not Arundel MS 57, the Morris or Gradon editions, the U-M digital encoding, or this repository's XML file.

contentParts.label: Author's Preface
contentParts.summary: Devotional opening, ownership statement, and prayers identifying Dan Michel, the title, and St Augustine's Abbey.

contentParts.label: Table of Contents
contentParts.summary: A prose guide to the chapter and leaf-reference system used in the manuscript.

contentParts.label: Moral and devotional treatise
contentParts.summary: The 127 encoded body sections covering commandments, creed, sins, virtues, confession, penance, prayer, and related doctrine.

dateStatements.display: Completed on the eve of Saints Simon and Jude, 27 October 1340
notes: The unique manuscript's colophon supplies a precise completion date for this English text.

dateStatements.display: First printed by Richard Morris in 1866

extent.description: The XML has one authorial preface, one table of contents, and 127 body DIV1 sections (129 DIV1 elements total). Among the 127 body sections, 125 are prose-only and two contain both prose and verse; the authorial preface contains three further verse groups. Counts describe the encoding, not a critical taxonomy of the medieval work.

formStatements.description: A prose devotional treatise with brief verse in the authorial framing and two body sections; the repository XML contains five line groups and 34 line elements, recorded at part level rather than making the whole-work discovery form mixed.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The work is a Kentish Middle English translation from French; the French source language is represented through the related-work relationship rather than counted as a language of this text.
The manuscript's date and location are unusually secure because its colophon, title statement, and BL catalog record converge.
Edition, encoding, access, and rights details are maintained in manifests/lineage/works/Ayenbite.json rather than inherited as properties of the medieval work.

next_steps: Consult a modern source study and a critical edition of Somme le Roi before recording passage-level adaptation claims.
openQuestions.question: Which passages in Ayenbite depart materially from, add to, or reorder the precise French exemplar used by Dan Michel?

notes: The BL catalogue locates production of the autograph manuscript at St Augustine's Abbey, Canterbury; this record does not infer that every stage of composition occurred there.
Dialect localization is a property of the Middle English text, distinct from the manuscript's present location.
The author role describes responsibility for the English work; the separate translator role preserves its dependence on the French source.

scope.description: Authorship of the Middle English translated work and its authorial framing
Kentish Middle English rendering of the French Somme le Roi

notes: This scoped source responsibility must not displace Dan Michel as creator of the Middle English translation.

scope.description: Authorship of the French source work, not direct authorship of the Middle English wording
1866 transcription and edition retained as the textual basis of the 1965 reissue
1965 corrected reissue and new collation of Morris's transcription
New collation against the unique Arundel 57 manuscript

summaries.value: A Kentish Middle English guide to conscience and moral life, translated by Dan Michel of Northgate from Laurent d'Orleans's French Somme le Roi. It organizes instruction around the commandments and creed, the seven deadly sins and corresponding virtues, confession and penance, prayer, almsgiving, chastity, sobriety, and the gifts of the Holy Spirit.

The work survives uniquely in Arundel MS 57, apparently in Dan Michel's own hand. The General Corpus text represents Gradon's 1965 corrected reissue of Morris's 1866 transcription and must not be treated as a diplomatic transcription of the manuscript.

notes: Dan Michel's authorial preface names the book in Middle English; spelling and capitalization vary in modern records.
An English gloss used in the bibliographic title of the Morris/Gradon edition.
Folger catalogs the edition under the uniform-title relationship to Laurent d'Orleans's Somme le Roi; this is not the preferred English work title.
